coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted polymers coated CdTe quantum dots with controllable particle size for fluorescent determination of p-coumaric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Ruiqing
Li, Te
Tong, Chaoying
Wu, Lihui
Shi, Shuyun
coredata.dc:description:
A facile and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on coating molecularly imprinted polymers onto the surface of CdTe quantum dots (CdTe-QDs@MIPs) was successfully fabricated for selective determination of p-coumaric acid (pCA) for the first time by the strategy of charge transfer principle. MIPs layer was synthesized by one-pot sol-gel reaction using 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) as functional monomer and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as crosslinker. Controllable particle size of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was formed by simply adjusting the polymerization time. Smaller particle size (about 56 nm) at polymerization time of 5 min reduced embedded sites, and then resulted in fast response (within only 4 min), and high sensitivity (limit of detection, LOD low to 6.74 μg L−1) for pCA. Different parameters during synthesis and determination procedures affecting the fluorescent probe were optimized. The fluorescent intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was remarkably quenched by pCA compared to CdTe-QDs@NIPs with an imprinting factor of 27.0. Finally, the fluorescence quenching analysis showed excellent linear ranges from 20 to 1000 μg L−1 (R
2 , 0.9964) with distinguished selectivity, and batch-to-batch relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.8%. The proposed method was applied successfully for the determination of pCA in pineapple juice and kiwi juice with satisfactory recoveries from 92.7% to 106.0%, and precisions below 8.1%. The proposed fluorescent sensor exhibited simple and rapid preparation and detection procedures, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. It is envisioned that the resultant CdTe-QDs@MIPs offered a new way of rapid and sensitive analysis of pCA in real complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdTe quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Fluorescent sensor
p-Coumaric acid
Fruit juice

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of pCA in fruit juices
Analytical figures for determination of pCA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of CdTe-QDs@NIPs (A), and CdTe-QDs@MIPs after (B) and before (C) removal of pCA.
Effect of polymerization time on fluorescence response and quenching efficiency (concentration of pCA at 100 μg L−1) of CdTe-QDs@MIPs.
PSA histogram of CdTe-QDs@MIPs by polymerization time at 5 min.
FT-IR spectra of TGA-capped CdTe-QDs (A), and CdTe-QDs@MIPs (B).
Fluorescence lifetime spectra of CdTe-QDs@MIPs in the absence and presence of pCA (A); UV–vis absorption spectrum of pCA and fluorescence emission spectrum of CdTe-QDs@MIPs (B).
Effect of the concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs (A), pH (B), and incubation time (C) on the response of CdTe-QDs@MIPs to pCA (concentration of pCA at 100 μg L−1).

Quenching efficiencies of pCA, FA, CGA, CA, VA, 4-HBA and PCA (each concentration at 100 μg L−1) on CdTe-QDs@MIPs and CdTe-QDs@NIPs (A), and the effect of store time on the response of CdTe-QDs@MIPs (B).
Fluorescence spectra of CdTe-QDs@MIPs (A) and CdTe-QDs@NIPs (B) upon addition of different concentrations of pCA (0‒1000 μg L−1), inset was the calibration graph.
Comparison of the developed method with other reported methods for pCA determination.
Recoveries of pCA in fruit juices by CdTe-QDs@MIPs based sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted polymers coated CdTe quantum dots with controllable particle size for fluorescent determination of p-coumaric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Efficient and Clean Utilization of Manganese Resources, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A facile and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on coating molecularly imprinted polymers onto the surface of CdTe quantum dots (CdTe-QDs@MIPs) was successfully fabricated for selective determination of p-coumaric acid (pCA) for the first time by the strategy of charge transfer principle. MIPs layer was synthesized by one-pot sol-gel reaction using 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) as functional monomer and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as crosslinker. Controllable particle size of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was formed by simply adjusting the polymerization time. Smaller particle size (about 56 nm) at polymerization time of 5 min reduced embedded sites, and then resulted in fast response (within only 4 min), and high sensitivity (limit of detection, LOD low to 6.74 μg L−1) for pCA. Different parameters during synthesis and determination procedures affecting the fluorescent probe were optimized. The fluorescent intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was remarkably quenched by pCA compared to CdTe-QDs@NIPs with an imprinting factor of 27.0. Finally, the fluorescence quenching analysis showed excellent linear ranges from 20 to 1000 μg L−1 (R
2 , 0.9964) with distinguished selectivity, and batch-to-batch relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.8%. The proposed method was applied successfully for the determination of pCA in pineapple juice and kiwi juice with satisfactory recoveries from 92.7% to 106.0%, and precisions below 8.1%. The proposed fluorescent sensor exhibited simple and rapid preparation and detection procedures, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. It is envisioned that the resultant CdTe-QDs@MIPs offered a new way of rapid and sensitive analysis of pCA in real complex samples.

ce:section.ce:para: p-Coumaric acid (pCA), a phenolic acid widely distributed in plants, has been of increasing interests due to its diverse physiological effects (e.g. antioxidant, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, antineoplastic, antimutagen, anti-Alzheimer, neuroprotective effects, UV-protection) [1–3] and wide range of applications (e.g. food, cosmetics, chemical, and pharmaceutical industries) [3,4]. Therefore, there is an increasing demand for the development of analytical methods for fast, accurate, and sensitive determination of pCA in real samples. Up to now, a variety of quantitative techniques have been developed including high performance liquid chromatography‒diode array detection (HPLC‒DAD) [5], HPLC‒mass spectrometry (HPLC‒MS) [6], HPLC‒electrochemical detection (HPLC‒ECD) [7], ECD [8], surface enhanced Raman (SER) [9,10]. Although these methods are robust and effective, in some cases, factors such as expensive instrumentation, sample pretreatment, long analysis time, and low selectivity restrict their practical and widespread use.

Recently, much attention has been paid to the fabrication of fluorescent sensor due to low cost, simplicity, and high sensitivity [11–14]. Quantum dots (QDs), one of the new fluorescent materials, have been widely exploited due to their outstanding optical and chemical features, such as facile preparation procedures, superior chemical and photo stability, modifiable nanoparticle surfaces, broad excitation spectra, narrow symmetric emission profile, high photoluminescence efficiency, and low background noise [12–14]. In terms of fluorescence detection, QDs sometimes simultaneously response to several species [11], therefore, the main limitation for QD based fluorescence sensors may be associated with their low selectivity to target compound. To improve selectivity, the surface of QDs should be modified by specific groups or materials [15].

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) contain memory function of shape, size and functional groups to template, which then have been regarded as the most potential artificial recognition materials for high selective extraction of template from complex matrixes [16–18]. Therefore, much attentions have been paid to the introduction of QDs capped by MIPs (QDs@MIPs) for the improvement of selectivity and sensitivity of QD based fluorescence sensors [12,13]. Generally, MIPs are designed and prepared via copolymerization of functional monomer and cross-linker in the presence of template by different methods (e.g. precipitation polymerization, bulk polymerization, suspension polymerization, and sol-gel polymerization) [19]. Among these ways, sol-gel process (e.g. reverse micro-emulsion method, Stӧber method) using 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as functional monomer and crosslinker, respectively, is the most popular strategy because of the facile synthesis process, and water-compatible capability [12–14]. As is known, the thickness and morphology of silica-based MIPs coating could affect the recognition behavior (e.g. the utilization of imprinting sites, adsorption rate) and the fluorescence intensity of QDs, which will then affect the sensitivity of QDs@MIPs [12,20]. Obviously, thin MIPs layer or small particle size of QDs@MIPs is promising. Previous sol-gel process always set polymerization time at 6–16 h [21,22]. As a result, the resultant particle sizes of QDs@MIPs are over 200 nm using Stӧber method, and the equilibrium time is 30–60 min [21], while using reverse micro-emulsion method, the particle sizes of QDs@MIPs are less than 100 nm, and the equilibrium time is less than 5 min [22]. By comparison, the Stӧber method is facile with fast polymerization rate. Then it could be concluded that smaller particles size for QDs@MIPs with rapid detection and high sensitivity could be achieved within shorter polymerization time using Stӧber method. However, relating works have not been reported, not to mention the sensitive detection of pCA based on QDs@MIPs.

To address the need for fast, sensitive and selective pCA detection, a novel fluorescence sensor based on MIPs on the surface of CdTe QDs (CdTe-QDs@MIPs) was fabricated through one-pot sol-gel imprinting procedure with polymerization time at 5 min. The relationship between polymerization time and particle size of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was investigated. Moreover, the application of CdTe-QDs@MIPs to detect pCA in real samples were investigated.

Tellurium powder, CdCl2·2H2O, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), thioglycolic acid (TGA), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) and anhydrous ethanol were analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Standards, pCA, ferulic acid (FA), caffeic acid (CA), cinnamic acid (CIA), chlorogenic acid (CGA), 4-hydroxybenzoic acid (4-HBA), and vanillic acid (VA), with purities over 99.0% were supplied by Xiya Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) by a water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

The morphology was evaluated by a Tecnai G2 20S-Twin transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Prague, Czech). The average values of the particle size and polydispersity of microspheres were determined by a particle size analysis (PSA) (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK). Infrared spectra were measured on a Nicolet-Avatar 360 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corp., Madison, Wisconsin, USA). Absorption spectra were collected by a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were assessed with a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., Washington, USA), and a Fluo Time 100 spectrofluorometer (PicoQuant, Berlin, Germany).

The rout for preparation of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was as follows. At first, TGA capped CdTe-QDs were synthesized according to previous report with some modifications [23]. Briefly, tellurium powder (25.5 mg) and NaBH4 (30.3 mg) were mixed with distilled water (2.0 mL) and stirred under nitrogen atmosphere. NaHTe precursor was successfully synthesized when transparent light purple solution formed. Meanwhile, CdCl2·2H2O (30.7 mg) and TGA (70.0 μL) were dissolved in distilled water (100.0 mL), and the pH value was adjusted to 9.0 using NaOH solution (0.5 mol L−1), which was then added into the NaHTe solution. Resulting mixtures were refluxed for 13 h at 90 °C under nitrogen atmosphere to obtain TGA-capped CdTe-QDs solution. After that, CdTe-QDs@MIPs were fabricated via one-pot sol-gel reaction using Stӧber method. Typically, pCA (0.35 mg) and APTES (20 μL) were dissolved in 80% ethanol solution (5 mL). After stirred for 30 min, TGA capped CdTe-QDs solution (10 mL), and TEOS (10 μL) were added and continuously stirred for 5 min. The products were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 1 min. The resultant precipitates, CdTe-QDs@MIPs, were washed with ethanol and water to remove pCA, and then dried under vacuum for 1 h.
As a control, CdTe-QDs@non-molecularly imprinted polymers (CdTe-QDs@NIPs) were prepared with the same procedures as those for CdTe-QDs@MIPs but in the absence of pCA.

The fluorescence spectra of CdTe-QDs@MIPs and CdTe-QDs@NIPs were carried out under the following conditions: excitation wavelength, 380 nm; emission wavelength, 600 nm; excitation and emission slit width, 10 nm; photomultiplier tube voltage, 900 V; spectral range, 550–650 nm. CdTe-QDs@MIPs and CdTe-QDs@NIPs were dispersed in distilled water to prepare freshly-made solution (0.8 mg mL−1), respectively. Then, CdTe-QDs@MIPs or CdTe-QDs@NIPs solution (0.3 mL), Tris-HCL buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0, 2.7 mL), pCA standard solution (30 μL, 20.0−1000.0 μg L−1) were fully mixed and incubated for 4 min at room temperature. Finally, fluorescence intensity of the mixtures emitted at 380 nm was recorded. Three replicates were performed for each measurement under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of pCA in fruit juices
ce:section.ce:para: Pineapple juice and kiwi juice (Beijing Huiyuan Beverage and Food Co., Ltd., China), purchased from local supermarket in Changsha, were selected to demonstrate the applicability of CdTe-QDs@MIPs for selective and sensitive quantitative fluorescence detection of pCA from complex matrices. Fruit juices were firstly filtered through a 0.22 µm polytetrafluoroethylene syringe filter to remove suspended pulp. Then, fruit juice (30 μL), CdTe-QDs@MIPs solution (0.3 mL, 0.8 mg mL−1), and Tris-HCL buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0, 2.7 mL) were mixed and incubated for 4 min, and then detected.

Silica was an ideal material for molecular imprinting due to its rigid structure, chemically inert, and hydrophilic capability. During the preparation of CdTe-QDs@MIPs, surface MIPs were synthesized by one-pot sol-gel polymerization using Stӧber method. Amino groups in APTES and carboxyl groups in TGA capped CdTe-QDs could interact with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in pCA through hydrogen bonds to form pre-polymerization complex, and then hydrolysis and condensation reactions of APTES and TEOS happened to imprint pCA in the composites. After removal of pCA, recognition sites were revealed. As shown in Fig. 1, fluorescent intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs after binding with pCA was relatively weak, and after removal of pCA, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was restored to almost the same (98.4%) with that for CdTe-QDs@NIPs. The results indicated that CdTe-QDs@MIPs were successfully synthesized, and template pCA was almost removed from the recognition sites.

The role of the MIPs size is very important due to its influence on the binding efficiency and sensitivity [22]. As is known, the thickness of silica layer could be simply tuned by adjusting the polymerization time [20,24]. As shown in Fig. S1, the particle size of CdTe-QDs@MIPs increased linearly with the increasing of polycondensation time (R 2 = 0.9880, slope = 2.11 nm/min). The fluorescence intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs and quenching efficiency of pCA (concentration at 100 μg L−1) to CdTe-QDs@MIPs with different polycondensation times were detected ( Fig. 2). Fluorescence intensity slightly increased when the polymerization time was less than 5 min, after that fluorescence intensity decreased obviously with the increasement of polymerization time because of the increased MIPs shell thickness. CdTe-QDs@MIPs with polymerization time at 5 min and particle size at about 56 nm showed the highest quenching efficiency. TEM image (Fig. S2) and PSA histogram ( Fig. 3) of CdTe-QDs@MIPs with polymerization time at 5 min exhibited spherical shape, while much amount of CdTe QDs was uniformly embedded in the spherical core.

The FT-IR spectrum of TGA-capped CdTe-QDs contained the characteristic peaks at 1211 cm−1 (C−H stretching), 1403 cm−1 (C˭O stretching), 1581 cm−1 (deformation vibration of carboxylic group), and 3426 cm−1 (O−H stretching) ( Fig. 4A). The disappearance of S−H stretching vibration peak at about 2500 cm−1 indicated that CdTe-QDs were successfully modified with TGA [11]. After polymerization imprinted, the occurrence of 1035 cm−1 (Si−O−Si stretching) and 772 cm−1 (Si−O vibration) (Fig. 4B) confirmed the successful sol-gel preparation of MIPs on CdTe-QDs.

The fluorescence intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was quenched by pCA binding. The occurrence of charge or energy transfer from CdTe-QDs@MIPs to pCA could be supported by the variable fluorescence lifetimes of CdTe-QDs@MIPs in the absence (17.65 ns) and presence (16.37 ns) of pCA ( Fig. 5A) [25]. The UV adsorption bands for pCA were 227, 300 sh, and 326 nm, and the emission wavelength of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was 600 nm (Fig. 5B). It was obvious that no spectral overlap existed between absorption spectrum of pCA and emission spectrum of CdTe-QDs@MIPs, which excluded energy resonance transfer as the possible quenching mechanism. Therefore, it could be concluded that the observed fluorescence quenching was attributed to the charge transfer from CdTe-QDs@MIPs to pCA.

To obtain a broad linear range with a high sensitivity and short analysis time in the quantification of pCA, various conditions (e.g. concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs, pH, and response time), affecting the response of CdTe-QDs@MIPs, were investigated and optimized.

The concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was of the utmost importance for fluorescence intensity, successful binding, and quenching efficiency. As in known, too low concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs would bring about a narrow linear range, while too high concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs would result in a low sensitivity [12,13]. Here, different concentrations of CdTe-QDs@MIPs (50‒200 μg mL−1) by adding pCA (100 μg L−1) were investigated ( Fig. 6A). The fluorescence intensity increased with the increasement of the concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs and reached a maximum value at 100 μg mL−1, and after that it decreased perhaps because of the slight agglomeration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs in higher concentrations. Highest quenching efficiency was achieved when the concentration of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was fixed at 80 μg mL−1.

The pH of solution played a vital role on the fluorescence intensities of CdTe-QDs@MIPs and the interactions between pCA and CdTe-QDs@MIPs. CdTe-QDs were synthesized under alkaline conditions, therefore, fluorescence intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs should be high under alkaline environment. However, pCA was ionized, and protonation of amino groups decreased at high alkaline conditions, which would decrease the binding of pCA to CdTe-QDs@MIPs, and then decrease the quenching efficiency [26]. At the same time, imprinting silica layer could be eroded at high pH values, resulting in surface defects. Therefore, pH values from 6.0 to 9.0 were carried out to investigate their effect on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs and the quenching efficiency in the presence of pCA (100 μg L−1) (Fig. 6B). The results showed that pH 7.0–8.0 resulted in the maximum and relatively stable fluorescence intensity, while pH 7.0 deduced the highest quenching efficiency. So, tris buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) was used for further experiments.

The effect of incubation time of CdTe-QDs@MIPs with pCA on the quenching efficiency was important to ensure the successful completion of the binding of pCA into imprinting cavities. The incubation time in the range from 0 to 50 min was studied. As illustrated in Fig. 6C, quenching efficiency of CdTe-QDs@MIPs increased up to 4 min, and remained equilibrium over a time period of 50 min. Therefore, incubation time was chosen as 4 min, which indicated that CdTe-QDs@MIPs could be used to rapidly detect pCA in aqueous solution. By contrast, the incubation time for QDs@MIPs with particle size over 200 nm (polycondensation time from 6 to 16 h) was 30–60 min [27,28]. The extraordinary fast response was attributed to the small particle size with thin MIPs layer where recognition sites were formed on the surface for rapid recognition.

The performance of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was further demonstrated by testing the specificity, one of the most important issues in developing optical sensors. Then six structural analogues (FA, CA, CIA, CGA, 4-HBA, and VA) with concentration at 100 μg L−1 were selected to compare their response to CdTe-QDs@MIPs. As shown in Fig. 7A, the quenching efficiency of pCA to CdTe-QDs@MIPs (F0/F, 1.27) was much higher than that for CdTe-QDs@NIPs (F0/F, 1.02), which verified that CdTe-QDs@MIPs had imprinted cavities for specific recognition of pCA. Furthermore, no remarkable differences of quenching efficiencies (all close to 1.00) were observed among FA, CA, CIA, CGA, 4-HBA, and VA to CdTe-QDs@MIPs and CdTe-QDs@NIPs. The results indicated that the recognition sites in imprinted cavities for pCA were not complementary to analogues. Then the fabricated CdTe-QDs@MIPs had a promising potential application for selective detection of pCA.

The stability of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was another crucial factor for sensor performance, which was evaluated in aqueous solution at 4 °C over a period of thirty days. As shown in Fig. 7B, the fluorescence of CdTe-QDs@MIPs slightly decreased, and the fluorescence intensity was 95.30% of its initial fluorescence response after thirty days. The remarkable stability might come from the robust protection of silica-based MIPs shell.

Reproducibility was also an important characteristic in the application of CdTe-QDs@MIPs. Five batches of CdTe-QDs@MIPs were prepared using the same procedures. Batch-to-batch relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 3.8%, which indicated that the CdTe-QDs@MIPs retained good reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical figures for determination of pCA
ce:section.ce:para: Method validation was performed with linearity, limit of detection (LOD, standard deviation of blank sample/slope of calibration equation at 3:1), limit of quantification (LOQ, standard deviation of blank sample/slope of calibration equation at 10:1), precision and specificity in the optimum conditions. To compare the recognition ability of CdTe-QDs@MIPs and CdTe-QDs@NIPs, pCA was added with concentrations from 0 to 1000 μg L−1. As shown in Fig. 8, the fluorescence intensities of CdTe-QDs@MIPs decreased with the increasement of pCA concentrations, and the decrease of fluorescence intensity of CdTe-QDs@MIPs was remarkably greater than that of CdTe-QDs@NIPs for a given pCA concentration. The fluorescence quenching followed Stern–Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + Ksv[C], where Ksv was the Stern–Volmer constant and [C], and [C] was the concentration of pCA. The plots of F0/F to [C] for CdTe-QDs@MIPs of CdTe-QDs@NIPs were presented in the inset of Fig. 7. Ksv for CdTe-QDs@MIPs was considerably larger than that for CdTe-QDs@NIPs, and the ratio of Ksv values (i.e. imprinting factor) was 27.0, which also indicated the presence of specific recognition sites on CdTe-QDs@MIPs for highly selective recognition of pCA. A good coefficient (R 2 = 0.9964) with a wide linear range (20‒1000 μg L−1) was obtained for pCA by CdTe-QDs@MIPs. Based on the results, LOD (6.74 μg L−1) and LOQ (22.23 μg L−1) were achieved, which proved that pCA can be sensitively detected using the fabricated fluorescent sensor. Intra-day RSD value for 5 replicate determination of pCA (100 μg L−1) was 0.6%, which indicated the high reproducibility. Inter-day RSD value for a six-day span determination of pCA (100 μg L−1) was 1.6%, which also presented the small signal variability. Compared with other reported quantitative methods for pCA [5–8,10,11] ( Table 1), the prepared CdTe-QDs@MIPs could be used for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of pCA. Accordingly, the developed method was sufficiently practical for quantification of pCA in complex matrix.

CdTe-QDs@MIPs based sensor was applied for quantitative determination of pCA in pineapple juice and kiwi juice, respectively. No sample pretreatment procedures (e.g. liquid-liquid extraction, solid-phase extraction) were required except for filtration. The standard addition recoveries of pCA fell in the range of 92.7–106.0% with excellent RSD values in the range of 0.4%‒8.1% ( Table 2). The results illustrated that the developed method was sufficiently accurate for selective determination of pCA in realistic samples. Finally, pCA was estimated in pineapple juice and kiwi juice at concentrations of 0 and 5509.63 ± 46.2 μg L−1, respectively.

A newly designed fluorescence sensor based on CdTe-QDs@MIPs have been successfully fabricated for rapid, sensitive and selective determination of pCA through charge transfer quenching mechanism. CdTe-QDs@MIPs were simply synthesized by anchoring MIPs layer on the surface of CdTe-QDs through sol-gel method. The particle size could be simply tuned by changing the polycondensation time, and the small particle size with thin MIPs layer and appropriate recognition sites largely shortened the response time and improved sensitivity. In addition, MIPs layer promoted the stability of CdTe-QDs. CdTe-QDs@MIPs were then successfully used for detection of pCA in fruit juices. The excellent properties of CdTe-QDs@MIPs showed the potential value to the selective recognition in food, biological, environmental, and clinical fields. Moreover, the present study provided a new sight to facilely fabricate other sensitive QDs@MIPs sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660181), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2018JJ1043), and Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety (2018TP1003).

ce:section.ce:para: All the authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2019.01.007.
